metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body wt as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participated in this study
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)
Six men (32 6 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 6 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participatedinthisstudy

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: Good health confirmed by comprehensive medical evaluation including history, physical examination, electrocardiogram, standard blood and urine tests

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - medications and smoking
methods_participants.value: Study performed during first 2 weeks of follicular phase of menstrual cycle in female subjects

evidence.quote: None of the subjects was taking regular medications or smoked tobacco
All subjects had a stable body weight for $2 mo
Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR 1000/w, Waltham, MA)
Plasma glucose concentration was determined by using an automated glucose analyzer (Yellow Springs Instrument, Yellow Springs, OH)
At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body wt
[6,6-2H 2 ]glucose (priming dose: 18 mmol/kg

methods_participants.label: Evening liquid formula - carbohydrate content
methods_participants.value: At 2000, liquid formula (Ensure) containing 250 kcal (40 g carbohydrates, 6.1 g fat, 8.8 g protein)

evidence.quote: liquid formula (Ensure; Ross Laboratories, Columbus, OH), containing 250 kcal (40 g carbohydrates, 6.1 g fat, and 8.8 g protein)

methods_participants.label: Glycerol isotope infusion - timing and duration
methods_participants.value: Fasted from 2000 until completion of study the following day; measurements at 14 h and 22 h of fasting

evidence.quote: and then fasted until completion of the study the following day

methods_participants.label: Isotope infusion equipment
methods_participants.value: Calibrated syringe pumps (Harvard Apparatus, South Natick, MA)

evidence.quote: All isotopes were infused via calibrated syringe pumps (Harvard Apparatus, South Natick,MA)

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling timing - baseline glucose and glycerol TTR
methods_participants.value: Chilled tubes containing EDTA for plasma fatty acid, glycerol, and glucose concentrations and enrichments

evidence.quote: Blood samples were collected in 1) chilled tubes containing EDTA to determine plasma fatty acid, glycerol, and glucose concentrations and glycerol and glucose enrichments

methods_participants.label: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR measurement method
methods_participants.value: Gas chromatography (Hewlett-Packard 5890-II) with heptadecanoic acid as internal standard

evidence.quote: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR were determined by GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard)

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors; post hoc comparisons using Student's t-test for multiple comparisons

evidence.quote: A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting. Significant F-ratios were followed by post hoc comparisons with the use of Student's t-test for multiple comparisons
A value of P #0.05 was considered to be statistically significant

methods_participants.label: Statistical software
methods_participants.value: JMP statistical software version 3.2 (SAS Institute, Carey, NC)

evidence.quote: Statistical analyses were performed by using JMP statistical software version 3.2 (SAS Institute, Carey, NC)

methods_participants.label: Isotope Infusion Protocol Restart - 22-Hour Fast
methods_participants.value: At 1000, all isotope infusions stopped; catheters kept patent by infusing 0.9% saline

evidence.quote: At 1000, all isotope infusions were stopped, and catheters were kept patent by infusing 0.9% saline

methods_participants.label: Plasma processing - timing
methods_participants.value: Plasma separated by centrifugation within 30 minutes of collection; stored at -70°C until analysis

evidence.quote: Bloodsampleswereplacedinnice,andplasmawasseparatedbycentrifugationwithin30minofcollection.Plasmawasstoredat270°Cuntilanalyseswereperformed
Plasmacatecholamineconcentrationsweredeterminedbyasingleisotopederivativeradioenzymaticmethod
55% of total energy as carbohydrate

narrative_results.statement: The relative increase in glycerol rate of appearance with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men

evidence.quote: However, the relative increase in glycerol R_a with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

narrative_results.statement: 87% of female pigs compared with only 22% of male pigs survive after 1 year of severe energy restriction

evidence.quote: For example, 87% of female pigs, compared with only 22% of male pigs, survive after 1 yr of severe energy restriction (46)

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 states isotope infusion stop at 1000 hours; pass_2 describes syringe pump equipment with no timing information; these are incompatible facts about different aspects
Pass_1 describes epinephrine at basal conditions; pass_2 describes fatty acid and ketone body changes during fasting. These are different metabolites and conditions

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: With continued fasting, plasma insulin concentration decreased in both groups (P < 0.05), but the relative decline was greater in women (≈40% decrease) than in men (≈20% decrease) (P = 0.07)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The increase in plasma fatty acid and ketone body concentrations (17, 34, 35) and the decrease in plasma glucose concentration (17, 34) that normally occur during short-term fasting (60–86 h) are greater in women than in men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma insulin concentration decreased more in women than in men with continued fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The increase in plasma fatty acid and ketone body concentrations during short-term fasting is greater in women than in men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: At 14 h of fasting, mean plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men (P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: With continued fasting, plasma insulin concentration decreased in both groups (P < 0.05), but the relative decline was greater in women (≈40% decrease) than in men (≈20% decrease) (P = 0.07)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma epinephrine concentration was twice as high in women than in men at basal conditions
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma insulin concentration decreased more in women than in men from 14 to 22h of fasting

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 contains participant age data for men; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains participant age data for women; Pass 2 is empty
Same fact about good health requirement; Pass 2 provides additional detail about evaluation components
Same inclusion criterion about no regular medications or tobacco smoking, expressed with minor wording differences
Identical statements about stable body weight requirement for ≥2 months
Same inclusion criterion about sedentary lifestyle with exercise <1 hour per week for ≥6 months; minor wording and abbreviation differences
Same fact about female menstrual cycle timing during follicular phase; minor wording differences
Same assay method (DEXA/DXA); Pass 2 includes specific equipment model information

Both describe insulin concentration measurement by radioimmunoassay (RIA). Minor wording differences in phrasing ('Assay - insulin concentration' vs 'Plasma insulin measurement assay') but same underlying fact.
Both describe glucagon concentration measurement by radioimmunoassay (RIA). Minor wording differences in phrasing but same underlying fact.
Both describe catecholamine concentration measurement by single isotope derivative radioenzymatic method. Minor wording variations ('radioenzymatic' vs 'Radioenzymatic method') are negligible.
Both describe glucose concentration measurement by automated glucose analyzer. Pass 2 includes manufacturer details (Yellow Springs Instrument) not in Pass 1, but same core fact.
Both describe glycerol concentration measurement by GC-MS. Pass 2 includes additional detail about internal standard ([2-13C]glycerol) not in Pass 1, but same core methodology.
Both describe fatty acid concentration measurement by gas chromatography. Pass 2 includes manufacturer and internal standard details not in Pass 1, but same core methodology.
Both describe pre-study meal timing at 1800 hours evening before isotope infusion study. Pass 2 includes additional compositional details (12 kcal/kg, macronutrient breakdown) not in Pass 1.
Both describe second meal timing at 2000 hours with liquid formula (Ensure). Pass 2 includes detailed nutritional composition (250 kcal, macronutrient breakdown) not in Pass 1.
Both describe catheter insertion at 0530 hours; pass_2 provides additional detail about catheter locations and purposes but same core fact
Both describe glucose isotope infusion start at 0630; pass_2 adds duration (210 minutes) and isotope specification; same core timing fact
Both describe glycerol isotope infusion start at 0830; pass_2 adds duration (90 minutes) and isotope specification; same core timing fact
Pass_1 states isotope infusion stop at 1000 hours; pass_2 describes syringe pump equipment with no timing information; these are incompatible facts about different aspects
Pass_1 describes second isotope protocol start at 1430 hours; pass_2 is empty; orphan fact from pass_1
Both describe glucose priming dose of 18 mmol/kg; pass_2 specifies isotope type but same dosing value
Both describe glucose infusion rate of 0.22 mmol/kg body weight/min; minor unit notation difference (body wt/min vs body weight/min); same fact
Both describe glycerol priming dose of 1.8 mmol/kg; pass_2 specifies isotope type but same dosing value
Both describe the same glycerol infusion rate of 0.12 mmol/kg body weight/min. Pass 2 adds isotope specification but the core dosing fact is identical.
Pass 1 contains pre-study meal macronutrient composition; Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 1 contains liquid formula composition details; Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Both report 6 men and 6 women. Pass 2 adds age details (32±4 yr men, 27±2 yr women, premenopausal women) which is supplementary demographic information, not contradictory.
Pass 2 contains fasting duration information; Pass 1 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 2 contains blood sampling timing for 14h fasting condition; Pass 1 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 2 contains blood sampling timing for 22h fasting condition; Pass 1 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 2 contains blood sample collection tube type information; Pass 1 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains specific blood tube type information for insulin/glucagon measurement
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains specific blood tube type information for catecholamine measurement
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains blood sample processing timing specification
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains blood sample storage temperature specification
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains measurement method details for plasma glycerol and glucose
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains statistical analysis method specification
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains statistical significance threshold
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains statistical software specification
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific fasting duration measurement (14 hours). No comparison possible.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific fasting duration measurement (22 hours). No comparison possible.
Same fact with only underscore vs space difference in tag formatting
Same fact; pass_2 uses abbreviated time notation (14h, 22h) instead of full text (14 hours, 22 hours)
Same core fact about sexual dimorphism in lipid but not glucose metabolism; pass_2 adds specificity about fasting conditions (basal and short-term)
Same fact about insulin decrease in women vs men; pass_2 specifies the time interval (14 to 22h) while pass_1 says 'continued fasting'
Pass_1 describes epinephrine at basal conditions; pass_2 describes fatty acid and ketone body changes during fasting. These are different metabolites and conditions
Pass_1 has content about fatty acid and glycerol concentrations at 14h fasting; pass_2 is empty
